behaviors.description: The review input is scoped to the lines that changed, not the full content of each modified file.
behaviors.justification: The core failure mode — 'code analyzes lines that didn't change' — is a local parsing logic problem. It's deterministic and can be caught by mocking the GitHub diff response with a well-formed unified diff format. The risk is only if GitHub's real diff format has quirks the mock doesn't capture, but standard unified diff format is stable.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the GitHub API to return a realistic diff containing both context lines (unchanged) and changed lines. Verify that when the code extracts the review input to send to the LLM, it excludes the unchanged context lines and includes only the actual changes. This tests the diff parsing and filtering logic without requiring real GitHub or LLM calls.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Unit test of diff parsing is sufficient; no module wiring or external calls needed.

behaviors.description: Inline comments are attached to the correct positions in the diff view on the pull request.
behaviors.justification: A real failure — 'comments appear at wrong line numbers' — requires testing the interaction between diff parsing and line number calculation across multiple modules. A unit test of just line number math might miss subtle issues with how diff context is handled. A pure mock might hide format quirks. However, you don't need to actually post to GitHub to verify the calculation is correct.
behaviors.test_description: Parse a realistic pull request diff (using real GitHub diff format). For each simulated issue, calculate the line number that should be reported to GitHub for diff-view positioning. Verify that the calculated line numbers correctly map changed code to the diff's line numbering scheme. This tests the full diff parsing and line-number mapping logic without calling the real GitHub API.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration test exercises diff parser + line mapper together; System test (real GitHub) is less essential because the code's job is to calculate line numbers, not verify GitHub's rendering.

behaviors.description: The agent identifies at least one real defect in a pull request that contains a known introduced bug.
behaviors.justification: This behavior explicitly depends on what a real LLM produces. Mocking the LLM's response would hide failures in prompt design, agent reasoning, or LLM capability. A pure in-process mock cannot tell you whether the agent actually identifies bugs — only a real LLM call can. This is the core 'agentic' capability.
behaviors.test_description: Create or use a pull request that contains a realistic, intentional bug (e.g., missing null check, unhandled exception, logic error). Execute the full code-review agent with a real LLM call (not mocked). Verify that the agent's output includes identification of the known defect. Repeat with multiple known bugs of different categories (null safety, error handling, performance, style).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Must be Agentic because the failure (missing bug detection) only manifests with a real LLM; mocks would hide it.

behaviors.description: Every finding listed in the top-level summary corresponds to an inline comment that was also posted — no findings appear in the summary without a matching inline comment.
behaviors.justification: A real failure — 'summary includes a finding that wasn't posted as an inline comment' — is a consistency/state-management bug. It requires testing multiple modules (LLM output handler, inline comment poster, summary generator) wired together. Mocking the LLM and GitHub API is sufficient; you don't need real external calls because the issue is in the code's orchestration logic, not in what the LLM produces or how GitHub behaves.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the LLM to return a fixed set of findings (e.g., 3 bugs, 2 style issues). Mock the GitHub API to record all posted comments. Test the code that orchestrates posting inline comments and generating the summary. Verify that the summary text contains references to exactly the findings that were also posted as inline comments, with no orphaned findings in the summary.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration test (multiple modules + orchestration) is sufficient; the failure is in code consistency logic, not in real LLM capability or GitHub API edge cases.

behaviors.description: The full sequence — reading the diff, posting inline comments, posting the summary comment — completes correctly for a real pull request without manual intervention.
behaviors.justification: A real failure — 'workflow fails or stalls midway' — can only be caught by running the full sequence with real components. Failures that manifest only in the full orchestration (e.g., LLM timeout causing retry logic to fail, GitHub rate limiting between comment posts, state corruption between steps) would not be caught by testing individual steps or components in isolation. The behavior explicitly requires 'for a real pull request' and 'without manual intervention,' indicating that real GitHub and real LLM are non-negotiable.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real pull request in a test GitHub repository with intentional issues in the code. Execute the full agent workflow with real GitHub API calls and real LLM calls: (1) fetch the diff, (2) call LLM to review, (3) post inline comments, (4) post summary comment. Verify that all steps complete end-to-end, no timeouts occur, state is correctly passed between steps, and the pull request shows both inline comments and summary comment.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Workflow test is necessary because individual Agentic or System tests would miss failures that only emerge in the full orchestration (state passing, retry logic, timeout handling across multiple steps).
